raw_case:
A novel nonsense RPS26 mutation in a patient with Diamond–Blackfan anemia: a case report


Background

Diamond–Blackfan anemia is a rare congenital disorder characterized by erythroid hypoplasia and is associated with mutations in ribosomal protein genes. This case report describes a novel variant in the RPS26 gene, which, to our knowledge, has not been previously documented. Reporting this case adds to the understanding of Diamond–Blackfan anemia’s genetic diversity and phenotypic manifestations.

Case presentation

A 16-month-old Turkish girl presented with pallor and macrocytosis. There was no familial history of anemia. Hemoglobin electrophoresis showed hemoglobin F at 10.8%, hemoglobin A2 at 1.7%, and hemoglobin A at 87.5% (normal range 0–2%). Peripheral smear demonstrated macrocytosis and reticulocytopenia. Bone marrow examination revealed marked erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis. Targeted next-generation sequencing, which included genes such as RPL11, RPL15, RPL26, RPL35A, RPL5, RPS10, RPS17, RPS19, RPS24, RPS26, RPS28, RPS29, RPS7, and TSR2, identified a heterozygous c.221G>T (p.C74F) variant in the RPS26 gene. This variant is reported here for the first time.

Conclusions

The identification of the c.221G>T (p.C74F) variant in RPS26 provides new insights into the genetic underpinnings of Diamond–Blackfan anemia. This finding underscores the importance of genetic testing in diagnosing Diamond–Blackfan anemia and highlights the potential for new mutations to contribute to the clinical presentation of the disease. Further research into RPS26 mutations may enhance the understanding of Diamond–Blackfan anemia’s pathogenesis and lead to improved diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

Introduction

Diamond–Blackfan anemia (DBA) is a rarely diagnosed congenital erythroid hypoplasia [1]. Ribosomal dysfunction is commonly seen in DBA; consequently, patients often present with hypoplastic anemia and congenital anomalies [2]. Characteristically, macrocytic anemia and reticulocytopenia are detected in DBA. Moreover, the risk of malignancy is increased in these patients. Hematological complications are detected by 1 year of age in 90% of affected individuals [3]. The malignancies to which patients with DBA are predisposed include acute myeloid leukemia (AML), myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), and osteogenic sarcoma [4]. Patients with DBA often have elevated erythrocyte adenosine deaminase (eADA) and hemoglobin F levels [5]. The condition is mostly inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, though 55–60% of cases occur sporadically [6]. The incidence of DBA is estimated at 1–4 per 500,000 per year [7]. DBA is categorized as a ribosomopathy [8] and is characterized by mutations in either the ribosomal protein large (RPL) or ribosomal protein small (RPS) subunit genes [9]. Mutations have been identified in nine of the ribosomal genes: RPS19, RPL5, RPS26, RPL11, RPL35A, RPS10, RPS24, RPS26, RPS28, RPS29, RPS7, and RPS17 [10]. RPS26 is an essential component of mRNA processing. The detection of mutations in the RPS26 gene affects the small subunit of the ribosome, which can lead to significant clinical manifestations. Currently, 30 variants reported to cause DBA are known [11, 12]. Here, we present a patient with DBA according to the genetic test results. Notably, this is the first report of the c.221G>T (p.C74F) variant in the RPS26 gene.

Case report

A 16-month-old Turkish girl presented with pallor. The family history was unremarkable. Physical examination did not reveal hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests indicated hemoglobin (Hb) at 5.2 g/dL (normal range 10.8–14.6 g/dL), red blood cell (RBC) count of 1.41 × 106/microliter (reference range 3.97–5.01 × 106/microliter), and reticulocyte count of 1.43% (reference 2–6%). Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) was found to be 115.5 fL (normal range 71.3–82.6 fL). White blood cell (WBC) and platelet counts were within normal limits. Bone marrow examination revealed marked erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis, while the granulocyte and megakaryocyte lineages appeared normal. Testing for blood serum antibodies, including cytomegalovirus immunoglobulin (Ig)M, parvovirus B19, herpes simplex virus IgM, and toxoplasma virus IgM, yielded negative results. No hemolysis was detected, as indicated by the lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level of 327 U/L, total bilirubin of 0.45 mg/dL, direct bilirubin of 0.09 mg/dL, and a negative direct Coombs test. Hemoglobin electrophoresis revealed HbF at 10.8%, Hb A2 at 1.7%, and HbA at 87.5% (normal range 0–2%). No thymoma was identified on X-ray and tomography. The patient had no history of drug use and was subsequently diagnosed with DBA. Steroid treatment was initiated, and the patient exhibited a positive response. The patient’s genomic DNA was studied using next-generation sequencing (NGS) on the MiSeq platform (Illumina). The test screened the RPL11, RPL15, RPL26, RPL35A, RPL5, RPS10, RPS17, RPS19, RPS24, RPS26, RPS28, RPS29, RPS7, and TSR2 genes for point mutations and small genomic deletions or insertions with over 99% sensitivity. The variants were analyzed with Sequencing Analysis Viewer (SAV) Software from Illumina and The Integrative Genomics Viewer (IGV) based on pathogenicity scores, in silico prediction tools, and genotype–phenotype correlation. After NGS analysis, a heterozygous c.221G>T (p.C74F) variant was detected in the RPS26 gene (Fig. 1). This mutation was confirmed as a de novo mutation by NGS, indicating that neither of the patient’s parents were carriers of the mutation. This variant has been interpreted as “pathogenic” according to the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics (ACMG)’s variant guidelines. We report a de novo variant in our patient, summarizing that this heterozygous variant in RPS26 is highly likely to contribute to DBA in this case.

Discussion

Diamond–Blackfan anemia is caused by defective ribosome biogenesis due to heterozygous pathogenic variants in ribosomal protein (RP) genes. A decreased number of functional ribosomes leads to the activation of pro-apoptotic pathways and reduced translation of genes essential for erythropoiesis [13]. In DBA, mutations in the RPS26 gene are found in 5.3–11.6% of cases [11]. Mutations affecting the RPs of both small (RPS24, RPS17, RPS19, RPS10, RPS26, RPS7) and large (RPL35A, RPL5, RPL11, RPL26) ribosomal subunits have been identified in patients with DBA [14]. Chae et al. reported heterozygous mutations in RPS19, RPS26, and RPS17 in seven patients with DBA [15]. GATA1, TSR2, and RPS26 mutations may also be observed in DBA [16]. In the study conducted by Wan et al., a correlation was noted between RPL11 or RPS26 mutations and the risk of short stature in patients with DBA [17]. Gripp et al. identified pathogenic RPS26 mutations in two of six families in their study [18] . In our case, NGS was performed, revealing a heterozygous variant in the RPS26 gene (c.221G>T), which results in the amino acid change p.C74F. Sanger sequencing confirmed that neither of the patient’s parents were carriers of this mutation. This represents a de novo variant in a family with no previous history of the disease. Therefore, we conclude that this heterozygous variant in RPS26 likely contributes to the DBA phenotype in this patient.

Conclusion

Mutations in the RPS26 gene may be responsible for the DBA phenotype in this case. The hematological outcomes and congenital anomalies observed are likely a result of abnormal ribosome biogenesis stemming from the associated mutation.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 16-month-old Turkish female
- **Chief Complaint**: Pallor
- **History of Present Illness**: Macrocytosis and reticulocytopenia were identified.
- **Past Medical History**: None
- **Personal History**: No history of drug use
- **Family History**: No familial history of anemia
- **Physical Examination**: No hepatosplenomegaly noted
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Laboratory findings: 
    - Hemoglobin: 5.2 g/dL (low) 
    - RBC count: 1.41 × 10⁶/μL (low)
    - Reticulocyte count: 1.43% (low)
    - MCV: 115.5 fL (elevated)
  - Bone marrow exam: Marked erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis
  - Peripheral smear: Macrocytosis
  - Hemoglobin electrophoresis: HbF 10.8%, HbA2 1.7%, HbA 87.5%
  - Negative testing for cytomegalovirus, parvovirus B19, herpes simplex virus, and toxoplasma virus antibodies
  - No hemolysis: LDH 327 U/L, total bilirubin 0.45 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.09 mg/dL, negative direct Coombs test.
  - Imaging: No thymoma on X-ray or tomography
  - Genetic testing: Next-generation sequencing revealed a previously unreported heterozygous c.221G>T (p.C74F) mutation in the RPS26 gene. Sanger sequencing confirmed de novo occurrence.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**: A young child presenting with normocytic or macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, and erythroid hypoplasia warrants evaluation for congenital bone marrow failure syndromes, acquired aplastic anemia, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or hemolytic anemias.
   - Nutritional deficiencies (e.g., B12 or folate deficiency): Excluded based on laboratory findings and lack of supporting history.
   - Hemolytic anemias: Excluded due to normal LDH, bilirubin, and negative Coombs test.
   - Infections (e.g., parvovirus B19): Excluded via negative antibody testing and absence of relevant symptoms.
   - Aplastic anemia: Excluded via normal granulocyte and megakaryocyte lineages on bone marrow examination.
2. **Focus on Congenital Disorders**:
   - Diamond–Blackfan anemia (DBA): Strongly suspected due to macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, erythroid hypoplasia, and elevated HbF levels.
   - Fanconi anemia: Less likely, as no physical malformations or chromosomal breakage analysis is provided to confirm this.
3. **Genetic Confirmation**: Next-generation sequencing identified a novel nonsense mutation (c.221G>T, p.C74F) in the RPS26 gene, consistent with DBA. De novo occurrence further supports the diagnosis in this patient with no family history of the disease. Mutations in the RPS26 gene are known contributors to DBA and were confirmed pathogenic by ACMG guidelines.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Diamond–Blackfan anemia associated with a novel heterozygous c.221G>T (p.C74F) mutation in the RPS26 gene.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's information. She's a 16-month-old Turkish female presenting with pallor. The key lab findings are macrocytosis and reticulocytopenia. So, her red blood cells are larger than normal, and she's not producing enough reticulocytes, which points towards a problem in the bone marrow's ability to make red blood cells.

First, I need to consider the differential diagnosis for macrocytic anemia with low reticulocytes. Common causes in children might include nutritional deficiencies like B12 or folate deficiency, but given her age, those are less likely unless there's a dietary issue. However, the family history doesn't mention any such issues, and she's not on any medications. The absence of hepatosplenomegaly on physical exam makes some chronic hemolytic anemias less likely, like hereditary spherocytosis. Also, the direct Coombs test is negative, ruling out autoimmune hemolytic anemia. The LDH and bilirubin levels are normal, so there's no evidence of hemolysis here.

Next, the bone marrow exam showed erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis. This suggests that the bone marrow isn't producing enough red blood cell precursors, and the ones that are present are abnormal. This is a key point. Erythroid hypoplasia can be seen in conditions like Diamond-Blackfan anemia (DBA), which is a congenital pure red cell aplasia. DBA is typically associated with macrocytosis and reticulocytopenia. The age of presentation here fits, as DBA often presents in infancy.

Looking at the genetic testing, there's a heterozygous mutation in the RPS26 gene. I recall that DBA is commonly linked to mutations in ribosomal protein genes, including RPS19, RPS26, and others. The fact that this is a de novo mutation (confirmed by Sanger sequencing) supports the diagnosis of DBA since many cases are sporadic. Other inherited bone marrow failure syndromes like Fanconi anemia usually present with pancytopenia and have different genetic mutations. The absence of physical anomalies, which are sometimes seen in DBA (like thumb abnormalities), isn't mentioned here, but not all cases have them.

Additionally, they ruled out infections like parvovirus B19, which can cause transient aplastic crisis, but the antibody tests were negative. The hemoglobin electrophoresis shows elevated HbF (10.8%), which is another clue. In DBA, HbF levels are often elevated because of the stress on erythropoiesis. The HbA2 is normal, which helps rule out beta-thalassemia, where HbA2 is typically elevated. The MCV is very high (115.5 fL), which is more consistent with DBA than other anemias that might have macrocytosis but not as severe.

Putting this all together: the combination of macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, erythroid hypoplasia with dyserythropoiesis, absence of other causes like infections or nutritional deficiencies, and the presence of a de novo mutation in a ribosomal protein gene (RPS26) points strongly to Diamond-Blackfan anemia. Other possibilities like transient erythroblastopenia of childhood (TEC) usually present later (after 1 year) and have a different genetic profile. The genetic mutation here is key because RPS26 mutations are known in DBA.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient exhibits macrocytic anemia (MCV 115.5 fL, Hb 5.2 g/dL) with reticulocytopenia (1.43%), indicating impaired erythropoiesis. The absence of hemolysis (normal LDH, bilirubin, negative Coombs) and hepatosplenomegaly rules out hemolytic or sequestration causes.  
<step 2> Bone marrow findings of erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis suggest a primary bone marrow failure disorder. The lack of nutritional deficiencies (no history of malabsorption/drug use) and negative viral testing (parvovirus B19, CMV) exclude acquired causes of red cell aplasia.  
<step 3> The identification of a de novo heterozygous RPS26 mutation is critical. Mutations in ribosomal protein genes (e.g., RPS19, RPS26) are hallmark features of Diamond-Blackfan anemia (DBA), a congenital pure red cell aplasia. The elevated HbF (10.8%) further supports DBA, as persistent fetal hemoglobin is common in this disorder.  
<step 4> The absence of thymoma (imaging) and family history of anemia, coupled with the de novo mutation, aligns with sporadic DBA rather than inherited syndromes like Fanconi anemia (which typically involves pancytopenia and chromosomal breakage).  

### Answer:
Diamond-Blackfan anemia (due to RPS26 mutation)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient exhibits macrocytic anemia (MCV 115.5 fL, Hb 5.2 g/dL) with reticulocytopenia (1.43%), indicating impaired erythropoiesis. The absence of hemolysis (normal LDH, bilirubin, negative Coombs) and hepatosplenomegaly rules out hemolytic or sequestration causes.

Bone marrow findings of erythroid hypoplasia and dyserythropoiesis suggest a primary bone marrow failure disorder. The lack of nutritional deficiencies (no history of malabsorption/drug use) and negative viral testing (parvovirus B19, CMV) exclude acquired causes of red cell aplasia.

The identification of a de novo heterozygous RPS26 mutation is critical. Mutations in ribosomal protein genes (e.g., RPS19, RPS26) are hallmark features of Diamond-Blackfan anemia (DBA), a congenital pure red cell aplasia. The elevated HbF (10.8%) further supports DBA, as persistent fetal hemoglobin is common in this disorder.

The absence of thymoma (imaging) and family history of anemia, coupled with the de novo mutation, aligns with sporadic DBA rather than inherited syndromes like Fanconi anemia (which typically involves pancytopenia and chromosomal breakage).

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: A young child presenting with normocytic or macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, and erythroid hypoplasia warrants evaluation for congenital bone marrow failure syndromes, acquired aplastic anemia, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or hemolytic anemias.
Step 2: Nutritional deficiencies (e.g., B12 or folate deficiency) are excluded based on laboratory findings and lack of supporting history.
Step 3: Hemolytic anemias are excluded due to normal LDH, bilirubin, and negative Coombs test.
Step 4: Infections (e.g., parvovirus B19) are excluded via negative antibody testing and absence of relevant symptoms.
Step 5: Aplastic anemia is excluded via normal granulocyte and megakaryocyte lineages on bone marrow examination.
Step 6: Diamond–Blackfan anemia (DBA) is strongly suspected due to macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, erythroid hypoplasia, and elevated HbF levels.
Step 7: Fanconi anemia is less likely as no physical malformations or chromosomal breakage analysis is provided to confirm this.
Step 8: Next-generation sequencing identifies a novel nonsense mutation (c.221G>T, p.C74F) in the RPS26 gene, consistent with DBA.
Step 9: De novo occurrence further supports the diagnosis in this patient with no family history of the disease.

Step 10: Mutations in the RPS26 gene are known contributors to DBA and were confirmed pathogenic by ACMG guidelines. Final diagnosis: Diamond–Blackfan anemia associated with a novel heterozygous c.221G>T (p.C74F) mutation in the RPS26 gene.
